full_response:
To show that a decision problem π is in the complexity class NP we must exhibit  

1. a polynomial-length certificate (also called a witness) whose existence certifies that the answer to the instance is YES, and  
2. a deterministic polynomial-time verifier that, given the instance and the certificate, accepts exactly those instance / certificate pairs that correspond to YES-instances.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Instance of HAPPY-FRIEND
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• positive integers n (number of friends) and m (number of gifts);  
• for every friend i ∈ {1,…,n} a set S_i ⊆ {1,…,m} of gifts that makes that friend happy;  
• a target number h (1 ≤ h ≤ n).

YES-instance: there exists a subset F ⊆ {1,…,n} with |F| ≥ h such that the sets {S_i : i ∈ F} are pairwise disjoint (so we can actually hand out the requested gifts) and therefore all friends in F can be made happy simultaneously.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Certificate
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A convenient certificate contains the following two pieces of information:

1. The identities of the friends that are claimed to be made happy:  
   an index list (i₁,i₂,…,i_h) with all indices distinct and taken from {1,…,n}.  
   (Size ≤ h log n ≤ n log n bits.)

2. The proposed assignment of gifts:  
   for every listed friend i_j, the verifier already knows S_{i_j}; we therefore do not need to write S_{i_j} into the certificate again.  
   No other information is necessary—the instance itself contains every S_i.

Total certificate size is O(h log n) ⊆ O(n log n) ⊆ poly(n,m).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Polynomial-time verifier V
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Input: instance ⟨n,m,{S_i},h⟩ and certificate C = (i₁,…,i_h).

1. Check that all i_j are distinct and 1 ≤ i_j ≤ n; if not, reject.  
   (O(h) ⊆ O(n) time.)

2. Check |C| ≥ h; if not, reject.

3. Build a Boolean array used[1…m] initialised to FALSE.

4. For every friend index i_j in C  
      For every gift g ∈ S_{i_j}  
          if used[g] = TRUE then reject   ⟨two listed friends want the same gift⟩  
          else used[g] ← TRUE.

Running time of step 4 is Σ_{j=1}^{h} |S_{i_j}| ≤ Σ_{i=1}^{n} |S_i| ≤ nm, i.e. polynomial.

5. If no rejection occurred, accept.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Correctness
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• If the verifier accepts, then no two chosen friends request the same gift, hence the gifts can be distributed exactly as demanded, so at least h friends can be made happy.  
• If the instance is a YES-instance, there exists a subset F of at least h friends with pairwise-disjoint demand sets; writing those friends’ indices as the certificate causes the verifier to accept.  
Therefore the verifier accepts exactly the YES-instances.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Running-time bound
--------------------------------------------------------------------
All steps run in O(n + Σ_i |S_i|) ⊆ O(nm) ⊆ polynomial(n,m), satisfying the NP requirement.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
HAPPY-FRIEND has a polynomial-length witness and a deterministic polynomial-time verifier; hence it belongs to NP.

\boxed{\text{HAPPY-FRIEND}\in\text{NP}}  
Confidence: 0.37
